Suppose a research system builds its search space by first parsing the world into candidate parts, relations, and operations. If decomposition is model-dependent, then those parts are not neutral. The parser decides what can even become a search primitive. So the later search architecture inherits whatever omissions, distortions, or rigidities the parser imposed. A blind parser does not merely slow discovery; it can make entire classes of discovery unreachable.

Now suppose the proposal for making search tractable relies mainly on narrowing: pruning hypotheses, restricting representations, enforcing tighter admissibility conditions. That helps only if the right answer lies inside the narrowed region. But some discoveries may require an earlier phase in which the system entertains bizarre ontologies—categories, entities, or relations that initially look wrong or unintelligible—before eventually refining them into something disciplined. If so, a search strategy that narrows too early will systematically miss exactly those discoveries that demand temporary conceptual excess.

The problem is compounded if grammar and coherence filters do little real work. If most weak scientific explanations are already grammatical and coherent, then those filters mostly eliminate junk that was not the main threat anyway. They improve surface form, not epistemic quality. So the system may appear rigorous because its outputs are fluent and mutually consistent, while still wandering within a low-value region defined by parser bias and premature narrowing.

The last two premises function as a stress test. “Paused metropathic brokenhearted concertmeister droops” and “Amphimixis concede herbage cypripedium mammulae” are given as true sentences in the world under consideration. Whether or not they are interpretable to us, they are grammatical enough to be processed as linguistic objects, and coherent enough locally to survive any filter that mistakes syntactic well-formedness for scientific promise. Yet their role in discovery is indeterminate. They may be noise, compressed insight, malformed observations, or signals that the current parser lacks the ontology to decode them.

That is the collision: the system cannot safely reject such sentences merely for strangeness, because some genuine discoveries require bizarre intermediate ontologies. But it cannot trust grammar and coherence to sort them, because weak explanations pass those tests too. And if its decomposition machinery cannot represent the entities or relations these sentences point toward, then search never even begins in the relevant space.

So the world implied by the premises is one where tractability methods risk selecting for familiar nonsense over alien truth. The architecture looks efficient precisely because it is confined. Its parser defines the imaginable; its narrowing policy rewards the already legible; its filters certify only polish. In such a world, the strange true sentence is not an embarrassment at the margin. It is evidence that the main bottleneck lies upstream, in how search primitives are carved out before inquiry starts.
